Name: Paddle Mixing When Wee-Weeing
Author: Agness Buya Yombwe
Information: painting | acrylic & stitchery on canvas | 60 × 90 cm

Name: Sketchy Dog
Author: Alex Gardega
Information:
sculpture | clay

The sculpture was installed next to »Fearless Girl« which in turn was installed in 2017 across Wall Street's »Charging Bull« in the Financial District of Manhattan. According to Gardega, the sculpture is an act of solidarity to Arturo Di Modico, the bull's sculptor. »Fearless Girl«, intended to be a comment on the gender pay gap and lack of women in leadership positions, supposedly hijacks and vilifies Di Modico's original intention of depicting America's resilience after the 1987 stock market collapse.

“It was made by a billion dollar financial firm trying to promote an index fund. It is advertising/promotion in the guise of art. That was my only point.” – Gardega

Name: compulsive self-surveillance
Author: Amanda Lindsey
Information: installation, durational performance | 96 mason jars, urine, plexiglass | 91.44 × 60.96 × 19 cm

Name: Art Piss (on money and politics)

Name: Piss Christ
Author: Andres Serrano
Information:
photograph from series »Immersions« | cibachrome print | 150 × 100 cm
The work, depicting a small plastic crucifix submerged in a glass of the artist's urine generated much controversy and was vandalized multiple times, based on assertions that it is blasphemous.
“What it symbolizes is the way Christ died: the blood came out of him but so did the piss and the shit. Maybe if Piss Christ upsets you, it's because it gives some sense of what the crucifixion actually was like...I was born and raised a Catholic and I've been a Christian all my life.” – Serrano
Andres Serrano was blessed by Pope Francis among a group of artists in 2023.

Name: Oxidation
Author: Andy Warhol
Information:
painting from series »Oxidation Paintings« | copper metallic pigment & urine on canvas | 101.6 × 76.2 cm
The paintings were created by Warhol's assistants and »ghost pissers«, supposedly men scouted in New York's public baths, peeing or pouring urine on unstretched canvases, which were laid on the floor and coated in wet copper paint.
The abstract patterns, reminiscent of Jackson Pollock‘s action painting, are caused by the oxidization of the copper pigment. The chemical reaction is influenced by the person‘s diet and hydration, with some even taking vitamin B to deliberately influence the color of the painting.

Name: Fideicomissum
Author: Ann-Sofi Sidén
Information:
fountain | bronze
The fountain, comissioned by Sweden's Wanås Castle, is a self portrait of the artist dribbling a thin stream of water. The title refers to an obsolete, centuries-old Swedish aristocratic law by which property could be handed down only to male heirs. According to the artist, urinating echoes a primitive way to mark a territory and therefore the work infiltrates men occupying hugely skewed proportions of public space.
“So in the middle of everything, she’s squatting down, peeing, looking at this property, realizing that ‘this property would never, ever be given to me’,” – Vilde Horvei, research assistant

Name: Aurina #4
Author: Anne Noble
Information:
photograph from series »Piss Poles, Antarctica« | pigment print on silk paper | 78.6 × 99.7 cm
“My series, Piss Poles, Antarctica, (Aurina #1 – #6) 2008, is a collection of photographs of the yellow flags that mark approved sites for pissing outdoors at Antarctic field camps set up to support big science projects during the summer season. I often noticed them when I was out photographing the weather and was drawn to the frozen yellow puddles of urine at the base of the poles. I found the word Aurina, meaning both urine and the colour of gold in a dictionary of vulgar Latin.” – Anne Noble

Name: Pisser
Author: Anthony Goicolea
Information:
photograph | cibachrome print | 101.6 × 177.8 cm
This photograph is a self portrait of the then twenty-eight-year-old artist using makeup, costuming and digital editing to transform into multiple, identical barely pubescent boys engaging in immature, hedonistic or early erotic behaviour.
The work is inspired by Cindy Sherman's self portraits and deals with themes of adolescence, rebellion, sexuality and queerness, as well as exploration, transmutation of identity and narcissistic vanity.
“Someone once asked me, ‘If you saw yourself sitting alone in a bar, would you ask yourself out?’ It’s that sort of questioning of self-love and self-hate that I want to explore.” – Anthony Goicolea

Name: Piss Soap

Information: soap | used cooking oil, wood ashes & human urine

Name: Malade agonisant qui reçoit sur son visage l'urine d'une vache
Author: Bernard Picart
Information:
etching from book »Cérémonies et coutumes religieuses de tous les peuples du monde« | 14.7 × 20.4 cm
The etching displayed in the book »The Religious Ceremonies and Customs of All the Peoples of the World« shows a medical treatment recorded in India. A sick man is laid on top of a cow's back, so the urine streams over his face.

Name: Fill That Seat
Author: Bryan Buckley
Information:
performance [living sculpture]
The work, installed in front of the Lincoln Memorial, appears to be a statue, but rather consists of gold-plated actors standing perfectly still. The performer depicts Donald Trump urinating on the Supreme Court seat of the late Ruth Bader Ginsburg.
The performance, which lasted a few hours, mocks Trump's decision to push Amy Coney Barrett to the Supreme Court just days before the 2020 presidential election

Name: United we RAPE
Author: Carlos Latuff
Information:
illustration
This political cartoon features caricatures of U.S. President George W. Bush and UK Prime Minister Tony Blair and references the Abu Ghraib prison scandal of the Iraq war, during which detainees were raped and tortured by the military occupation.

Name: PISSED

Name: Three Sailors Urinating
Author: Charles Demuth
Information:
painting | watercolor & pencil on tracing paper | 21.6 × 23.8 cm
Here, the sailor acts as a queer icon denoting living, isolated from society, at sea in close proximity to other men, at a time where homosexuality was considered a sin and a capital offence. Historically, the Navy and ports of call became common places for homoerotic encounters and male camaraderie.
In Demuth's later artwork, Seamen appear more frequently as Demuth's object of erotic desire and as a secret, uninhibited outlet for his own sexuality, only circulated among his close confidants.
